title: Counting seals at False Creek
summary: Every Saturday morning, two volunteers count harbour seals in Vancouver. The numbers do not always agree.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: Good morning, David. How many today?

body.speaker: David
body.text: Good morning, Priya. I counted eleven near the docks and four under the bridge. That is fifteen.

body.speaker: David
body.text: [David checks his notebook.] We always have different numbers. Why?

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: Because you count from the east side and I count from the west side. We see different seals. We also see some of the same seals twice. The scientists know this. They take the higher number when we disagree, and they take the lower number when we agree.

body.speaker: David
body.text: How long have you been doing this?

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: Eight years. Every Saturday morning at seven. Rain, snow, holiday. Eight years.

body.speaker: David
body.text: And you have never missed a Saturday?

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: I missed one. My daughter was born on a Saturday. The other volunteer counted for me. She is in Toronto now, but I still owe her a count.

body.speaker: David
body.text: Why do you do it? Are you paid?

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: No. The scientists send me a yearly chart of the seal numbers. That is my payment. I keep the charts in a folder at home. There are eight charts now. I show them to my daughter.

body.speaker: Priya
body.text: She says, 'Mom, the seals are doing better than people.' [Priya smiles.] She is nine. She is correct.

annotations.meaningVi: cùng những con hải cẩu hai lần — đếm trùng
kẹp tài liệu / bìa hồ sơ
đang ổn hơn con người — câu nhận xét của cô bé
False Creek (cảng nhỏ ở Vancouver, Canada)
tôi còn nợ cô ấy một lần đếm
mưa, tuyết, hay ngày lễ (không vắng mặt dù thời tiết nào)

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect with 'never' and 'how long … ?'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: A2 dùng present perfect ('have/has + V3') để hỏi và trả lời về kinh nghiệm cho đến hiện tại. Trong bài: 'How long have you been doing this?' (ngụ ý: từ lúc nào đến giờ). 'And you have never missed a Saturday?' 'Never' đứng giữa 'have' và động từ thứ ba. 'I missed one' chuyển sang quá khứ đơn vì lần đó là một sự kiện cụ thể đã xảy ra. Quy tắc: present perfect cho kinh nghiệm chung; past simple cho một lần cụ thể.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A2 uses the present perfect ('have/has + past participle') for experiences up to now. From the dialogue: 'How long have you been doing this?' 'And you have never missed a Saturday?' 'Never' sits between 'have' and the past participle. Priya switches to past simple — 'I missed one' — because that is a specific completed event. Rule of thumb: present perfect for general experience; past simple for a specific past moment.

translationVi:
Priya: Chào David. Hôm nay được bao nhiêu?

David: Chào Priya. Tôi đếm được mười một con gần bến tàu và bốn con dưới cầu. Tổng là mười lăm.

Priya: Tôi đếm được mười bảy.

David: [David kiểm tra sổ tay.] Số của chúng ta luôn khác nhau. Vì sao?

Priya: Vì anh đếm từ phía đông và tôi đếm từ phía tây. Chúng ta thấy những con khác nhau. Chúng ta cũng đếm trùng vài con. Các nhà khoa học biết điều đó. Họ lấy số cao hơn khi chúng ta bất đồng, và số thấp hơn khi chúng ta trùng.

David: Chị làm việc này bao lâu rồi?

Priya: Tám năm. Mỗi sáng thứ Bảy lúc bảy giờ. Mưa, tuyết, ngày lễ. Tám năm.

David: Và chị chưa từng bỏ một thứ Bảy nào?

Priya: Tôi bỏ một lần. Con gái tôi sinh vào một thứ Bảy. Một tình nguyện viên khác đếm thay tôi. Cô ấy giờ ở Toronto, nhưng tôi vẫn còn nợ cô ấy một buổi đếm.

David: Sao chị làm việc này? Có được trả lương không?

Priya: Không. Các nhà khoa học gửi cho tôi biểu đồ hàng năm về số lượng hải cẩu. Đó là tiền lương của tôi. Tôi giữ các biểu đồ trong một bìa hồ sơ ở nhà. Giờ có tám biểu đồ. Tôi đưa cho con gái tôi xem.

David: Con bé nói gì?

Priya: Nó nói: 'Mẹ ơi, lũ hải cẩu đang khá hơn loài người.' [Priya cười.] Con bé chín tuổi. Nó nói đúng.

questions.prompt: What are Priya and David counting?
questions.explanation: 'How many today?' — they are counting seals.

questions.prompt: How many seals did David count?
questions.explanation: 'Eleven near the docks and four under the bridge. That is fifteen.'

options: They count from different sides (east and west); some seals are double-counted, others missed.

questions.prompt: What do the scientists do with the two numbers?
options: Higher number when the counts disagree, lower when they agree.

questions.prompt: How long has Priya been doing this?
questions.explanation: 'Eight years. Every Saturday morning at seven.'

questions.prompt: Why did Priya miss one Saturday?
options: Her daughter was born that day.
questions.explanation: 'My daughter was born on a Saturday.'

questions.prompt: How is Priya paid for her work?
options: She is not paid. She receives a yearly chart of the seal numbers.
questions.explanation: 'No. The scientists send me a yearly chart.'

questions.prompt: How many charts does Priya now have?
What does Priya's daughter say about the seals?
options: 'The seals are doing better than people.'
Citizen volunteers keep nature counted, week after week, paid only in evidence that the work matters.
Volunteers should be paid in cash.
Children should not see scientific charts.
questions.explanation: Priya's commitment and the chart-as-payment line.

cloze.template:
Priya: Good morning, David. How many today?
David: Good morning, Priya. I {{b1}} eleven near the docks and four under the bridge. That is fifteen.
Priya: I counted seventeen.
David: We always have different {{b2}}. Why?
Priya: Because you count from the east side and I count from the {{b3}} side. We see different seals. We also see some of the same seals twice. The scientists know this. They take the higher number when we {{b4}}, and they take the lower number when we agree.
David: How long have you been doing this?
Priya: Eight years. Every Saturday morning at {{b5}}. Rain, snow, holiday. Eight years.
David: And you have {{b6}} missed a Saturday?
Priya: I missed one. My {{b7}} was born on a Saturday. The other volunteer counted for me. She is in Toronto now, but I still owe her a count.
David: Why do you do it? Are you paid?
Priya: No. The scientists send me a yearly {{b8}} of the seal numbers. That is my payment. I keep the charts in a folder at home. There are eight charts now. I show them to my daughter.
David: What does she say?
Priya: She says, 'Mom, the seals are doing better than {{b9}}.' She is nine. She is {{b10}}.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Priya is paid in a yearly chart, not money. What does a tangible record of years of small work give you that a salary cannot — and is there work in your own life you would do for a chart?
